title: Transformation from Weaire-Phelan packing to thermally stable Euler crystals in polycrystalline face-centered cubic metals
author: Dan Yang
Xiaoming Su
Hongtao Xue
Xiuyan Li
Zhaohui Jin
abstract: Based on the least area principle to fill up space and Weaire-Phelan (W-P) conjecture, polycrystalline facecentered cubic metals Cu, Ni and Al consisting of triply-distributed coherent twin-boundaries were modeled at grain sizes below 10 nm. Atomistic simulations showed that grain boundaries in such W-P crystals may transform promptly into a unique structure characterized by three perpendicular catenoids of minimal surfaces formally proved by mathematician Leonhard Euler. The adopted Euler crystal can be thermally stable up to more than 80 $\%$ of the melting point. Densely embedded Σ3 {111} coherent twins are critical agents to trigger the transformation as well as to stabilize the resultant minimal-surface structures. Our results add further evidence that the evolution of 3-dimensional grain boundary networks into saddle-shaped minimal-surface morphologies may enhance the thermal stability of nanograined polycrystals.

main_letter.1: In nanostructured polycrystalline metals, all tiny pieces of crystals or nanometer sized grains are surrounded by segments of crystal interfaces or grain boundaries (GBs). The 3-dimensional (3D) GB morphology is a complex entity because both lattice orientation and shape of individual grains can be adopted randomly [1,2]. In addition, GBs themselves differ from each other both structurally and energetically and as intrinsically mobile planar defects, they may exhibit a variety of mobilities depending on temperatures, externally applied forces and the material [3]. It has been remained mysterious whether any unique 3D structures may possibly form to represent somewhat optimal polycrystals with an overall stability opposing collective structural changes, most notably, grain coarsening and grain growth [4].
main_letter.2: In topological terms, a polycrystalline structure can be treated as a face-edge-vertice (FEV) network connected by GBs (faces), edges or triple lines (TLs, where three GB planes meet) and vertices or quadrupolar points (QPs, where four TLs meet) [5,6]. The FEV-GB network is useful to characterize shapes of polyhedral grains. Alternatively, in terms of Voronoi diagram (or Dirichlet tessellation), an interpenetrating grain-face network can be specified to characterize a GB network associated with lattice relationship of neighboring grains. For example, if a chain of grains is connected by parallel coherent twin boundaries (CTBs) in one direction, we say that a channel (or tunnel) of CTB network is formed in that direction.
main_letter.3: The presence of twin-related low-index coincidence site lattice (CSL) boundaries and their influence to morphological changes have been investigated for a number of face-centered cubic (FCC) materials [7–11]. It is believed that twin boundaries in general improve the microstructural stability of polycrystalline metals. Most notably, it has been shown that introducing 3D CTB networks to neighboring grains of Cu is essential to trigger a structural transformation from a FEV-GB network to a unique metastable structure called ‘diamond Schwarz crystal (D-SC)’ upon annealing [12]. The D-SC structure is characterized by smoothly distributed 3D periodic minimal surface of Σ9-type general GBs as well as four channels of CTB-networks in each ${ < } 1 1 1 >$ direction of the cubic lattice. The structure was found extremely stable, exhibiting near-melting point thermostability and almost theoretical mechanical strength [12–14].
main_letter.4: The structural stability of Schwarz crystals is promising. Yet, it remains intriguing whether enhanced thermostability can be achieved by incorporating CTB networks of other forms in order to prevent GBs from developing substantial curvatures to escape. In this paper, rather than to examine a Kelvin polycrystal resembling the ‘ideal’ foam proposed by Lord Kelvin [15], we consider Weaire-Phelan (W-P) conjecture [16] as an optimal way to divide space and construct initial nanograined polycrystalline FCC metals of Cu, Ni and Al containing triply-distributed perpendicular CTB channels.
main_letter.5: Nanoscale microstructures indicating W-P tessellation have been observed experimentally such as in sputtering epitaxial growth of Pd/Pb monolayers [17] and in polymers due to polymerization induced phase separation [18]. Various Frank-Kasper phases beyond the packing rules known for metallic systems were also revealed with self-assembled molecules in soft matters [19]. These recent experimental observations suggest that W-P packing is not merely an interesting conjecture, but also represents a remarkable way to obtain optimized 3D microstructures in practice.
main_letter.6: # 2. Weaire-Phelan (W-P) packing The W-P model represents a complex 3D geometry of idealized foams of equal-sized bubbles. It is a better solution to the “Kelvin problem” than the Kelvin model [20,21]. The W-P structure uses two kinds of cells of equal volume, called “tetrakaidecahedron” (Tet) and “dodecahedron” (Dod), respectively, as shown in Fig. 1 (a). A W-P unit cell consists of three pairs of Tets and two sets of Dods. The center coordinates for each cell in a W-P construction follow the A15 Frank–Kasper phase [16]:
main_letter.7: (1) Corner Dod or Dod0, (0, 0, 0); center Dod or Dod1, $( { \scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } , { \scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } , { \scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } )$ .
main_letter.8: The unique arrangements of Tets allow us to introduce twin boundaries in 3D, as illustrated in Fig. 2 (a), while the lattice orientations of Dods, in principle, can be arbitrarily chosen. Initial W-P supercells in our MD simulations were built by considering both the size (d) and lattice orientations of those isovolumetric grains (Tets and Dods). The three pairs of Tets aligned in X, Y and Z directions allows one to introduce symmetric CTBs, such as Σ3 {111} or Σ5 $\{ 1 2 0 \}$ in X, Y and Z directions. Dods at the corner (Dod0) and at the center (Dod1) are of the same shape but perpendicular to each other. Obviously, the cubic orientations can be assigned to the two Dods to simplify the symmetric fit. In particular, we consider combinations of cubic Dods and twin Tets with those lattice orientations prescribed in Table 1.
main_letter.9: Lattice orientations assigned to W-P cells provided in Table 1 introduce Σ3 and Σ5 CTB networks. Clearly, for W-P crystals with Σ3 CTBs, GB segments of $< 1 0 0 > / < 1 2 1 >$ type (sometimes called incoherent twin boundaries, ITBs [9]) are formed between Dod0 and its neighboring Tets; whereas GB segments of $< 1 0 0 > / < 1 1 0 >$ type are formed between Dod1 and its neighboring Tets. It is expectable that the two types of asymmetric GB segments are not equally stable due to their different structures. The situation does not occur to W-P crystals with Σ5 CTBs, where all GBs segments are of ${ < } 0 0 1 >$ twist and asymmetric character, so, the structure is more uniform and energetically more isotropic.
main_letter.10: In this study, W-P cells of different sizes, i.e. $6 . 5 \mathrm { n m }$ , 10 nm, 15 nm and $2 0 \ \mathrm { n m }$ , respectively, were modelled and simulated. Due to the geometric (shape) symmetry of the W-P crystal, rotating each pair of Tets by 90 degrees around X, Y or Z axis results in essentially an identical set of GB network with exchanged positions between Dod0 and Dod1. Different GB networks can also be constructed by assigning different lattice orientations to Dods. In doing so, however, no W-P cell exhibiting superior thermal stabilities could be found according to our MD tests.
main_letter.12: All MD simulations were carried out with the molecular dynamics (MD) code LAMMPS [22]. The simulation system (a triclinic supercell) is periodic in all directions. Particle trajectories were integrated using the Nos´e-Hoover thermostat with a timestep of 2.5 fs. An isothermal isobaric ensemble (NPT) was used to maintain the zero-pressure condition. The heating procedure was conducted by increasing the temperature at intervals of 1 K at a heating rate of $8 ~ \mathrm { K } / \mathrm { n s }$ , similar to that in Refs. 12-14.
main_letter.13: The interatomic potential for Cu is based on a modified version of semi-empirical model (jin_copper_lammps.setfl) developed with the embedded atom methods (EAM) for Cu [23]. EAM potentials developed by Mendelev et al [24] is chosen to use for Ni and a force-matching potential based on density functional theory (DFT) calculations is chosen for Al [25]. Comparing to experimental data and DFT calculations [26–29], all the three sets potentials may reproduce fundamental material properties reasonably well, such as elastic constants, melting point and stacking fault energies (see Table S1 in supplemental data).
main_letter.14: The software Ovito [30,31] was used for analyzing and visualizing our MD data. With common neighbor analyses (CNA), atoms at stacking faults, twin boundaries and other defects can be distinguished from those at FCC lattice sites.
main_letter.16: # 4.1. Transformation from W-P to Euler crystal and thermal stability
main_letter.17: With Σ3 CTBs, structural evolution of W-P crystals into GB morphologies resembling Euler catenoids [32] are generally observed for MD samples of different cell sizes $\dot { \mathbf { } d } = 3 . 2 5 \mathrm { n m }$ , 5 nm, 7.5 nm and ${ 1 0 } \mathrm { n m }$ , respectively). Taken the case of 5 nm cell size as an example (Fig. 2 and supplemental movie clips), our observations may be summarized as follows.
main_letter.18: (1) The Dod at the corner (Dod0) retracts promptly at elevated temperatures and finally into a GB node or anchor at about $4 5 0 \mathrm { K } ,$ , a temperature (TWP-E, Figs. 2 and 3) at which the three pairs of twin Tets have transformed simultaneously into three ‘catenoids’ perpendicular to each other. The Dod at the center (Dod1), on the other hand, does not alter much. The transformation can be revealed by measuring the lateral spacing of CTBs in each pair of twin Tets or catenoids as a function of temperature, as shown in (2) Mathematically, a Euler minimal surface for a single vertical catenoid can be defined by [33–35]
main_letter.20: where $x , y ,$ and $\mathfrak { z }$ are coordinate positions and $\alpha$ is the shape factor (evolutionary parameter). Our MD observed catenoid configuration is found close to that described by Eq. 1 with $\scriptstyle \alpha = { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } .$ .
main_letter.21: (3) Preexsiting CTBs in each pair of Tets never tend to migrate away from their initial positions via mechanical twinning before and after the transformation. The shape of the CTB area may vary and deviate from the ideal circular one, manifesting their roles of dragging or impinging GBs bounding them. Our MD results reveal again that inhibiting grain growth by CTB networks depends (4) Increasing Σ3 coherent twin density within Tets in a W-P crystal (Fig. 4), e.g., double or twice-double the number of CTBs (noted as ‘twin $\times 4 ^ { \prime }$ and ‘twin $\times 8 ^ { \prime }$ , respectively), does not alter the way of transformation into a Euler crystal; instead, high-density twins tend to enhance the thermal stability, underlying a stronger ‘twin-fencing’ effect. As shown in Fig. 4 for Cu, enhanced thermal stability can be achieved by doubling the twin densities (twin $\times$ 4) in the W-P crystal $( a = 1 0 \ \mathrm { n m } )$ ), which is $\sim 0 . 8 ~ T _ { \mathrm { m } }$ (Fig. 4 (b)). Nearly the same thermal stability can be achieved by further doubling the twin density $( \arcsin \times 8 )$ . For both cases, W-P crystals were found capable to transform into Euler crystals and the transformation temperatures are nearly identical as illustrated in Fig. 4 (d) for Cu as well as for Ni and Al.
main_letter.22: (5) The Dod1 at the box center is also found bounded by additional CTBs, products of thermally activated GB reactions at temperatures above room temperature, as shown in Fig. 4 and 5. GB decomposing into twins constitute an important mode of GB activity accounting for grain growth or coarsening according to our MD observations.
main_letter.23: # 4.2. GB networks in Euler crystals (catenoids, chains and anchor) A Euler crystal consists of three identical catenoids in $X , ~ Y ,$ and Z directions in close contact. A complemental grain at the center bounded by portions of embedded catenoids is also necessary to fill up the space of a supercell (Fig. 5 and Fig. S1 in supplemental). As can be seen, the rims of the three catenoids are anchored at the corners of the cubic box but framed in such a way that from the anchor toward the box center, a triple line or a short chain connecting the anchor and the corner of the inner grain is formed. Accordingly, ideal Euler crystals can be built using three identical catenoids lying on X, Y, and Z planes respectively and packing them into close contact. As verified in our MD simulations (Fig. S1), the Euler crystals constructed this way are thermally as stable as those transformed from W-P crystals.
main_letter.24: Unlike W-P crystal with convex grain cells and flat GB segments, Euler crystals consisting of concave catenoids. In terms of a FEVnetwork, the minimal surface GBs of catenoids $( F = 1 8 )$ ) are connected by 32 triple lines (TLs) and 9 quadrupolar points (QPs). Among the QPs, eight of them are standard ones which are associated with Dod 1. The other one, on the other hand, is a ‘super’ QP where eight TLs or chains end up (Fig. 5 (b)). As an anchor of the three Euler catenoids linked by eight chains, such a super QP turns out to be extremely stable according to our MD observations.
main_letter.25: The ultimate thermal stability of polycrystals depends on GB networks at equilibrium, both locally and globally. Our MD observations show clearly that after transforming into a Euler crystal, the number of GB nodes or edges in a W-P crystal can be reduced significantly, such that an equilibrium GB network of zero mean-curvature catenoids, GB chains and anchor is formed opposing thermal fluctuations and grain coarsening at elevated temperatures.
main_letter.26: # 4.3. A counter example: W-P crystals with Σ5 CTBs
main_letter.27: No transformation to Euler catenoid was observed in our MD simulations if a W-P crystal is composed of GB network of other types, such as Σ5 or Σ9. Fig. 6 (a) illustrates such a case, a W-P crystal with Σ5 twin Tets $\dot { \boldsymbol { d } } = 5 . 3 \ \mathrm { n m }$ ). With this set of W-P crystals, we found that at elevated temperatures, instead of retracting into an anchor, the grain cell Dod0 neither changed its size nor its shape noticeably, while Dod1 underwent grain growth or coarsening at temperatures above the room temperature. The process of coarsening is continuous, initially slow and tends to be faster at temperatures beyond $4 0 0 ~ \mathrm { K } ,$ , such that the W-P structure collapses promptly at about $6 0 0 ~ \mathrm { K }$ when the area covered by Σ5 CTB in twin Tets shrinks and vanishes (Fig. 6 (b)-(c), see also the supplemental movie clip). Our MD observations revealed again that GBs enclosing a Dod may exhibit very different mobilities at elevated temperatures.
main_letter.29: Unlike the 2D or 3D foam geometry where the surface tension of bubbles can be treated approximately as everywhere uniform [34,35], an equilibrium GB network in polycrystals not only depends on the shapes of grains, but also the texture profile of neighboring grains [2]. The reason is simple, depending on the misorientation angle of neighboring grain lattices, GBs in a piece of polycrystal may show different atomic structures, excess energies and mobilities (either intrinsic or extrinsic).
main_letter.30: Texture effects on structural thermostabilities due to isotropic/ anisotropic GB energies and elastic mismatch of neighboring grain lattices have been considered in a recent paper for twin-free Schwarz crystals of different shapes and sizes [36]. For GBs resembling primary, diamond or gyroid triply periodic minimal surfaces, it is concluded that energetically less anisotropic GBs are expected to be thermally more stable. Similar considerations also apply to address the transformation from W-P to Euler crystal and the MD observed thermostability, although the present situation is much complicated due to that minimal-surface GBs (catenoids), CTBs, GB nodes (QPs) and GB chains (TLs) coexist in a Euler crystal.
main_letter.32: Adaptive GB migration play essential roles ensuring structural evolution from W-P crystal to minimal-surface Euler catenoids. In W-P crystals with Σ3 twin Tets, at least two different kinds of GB characteristics were introduced between Dods and Tets, which are {112} / {010} TD0 GBs between Dod0 and twin Tets and {110} / {001} TD1 GBs between Dod1 and twin Tets, according to Fig. 1 and Table 1. The TD0 GB, similar to incoherent Σ3 twin boundaries [9], happens to be mobile such that the cell Dod0 bounded by TD0 GBs may deviate promptly from its initial shape at elevated temperatures (Fig. 3 and 4). It retracts and finally merges into a GB node at the point of disappearance, anchoring the three twin catenoids simultaneously.
main_letter.33: At least three factors account for the distinct behaviors exhibited by the two Dods, GB structure, GB energy and modes of GB motion. In general, GB structures and the mode of GB motion, such as migration and sliding, are mutually coupled [37]. It is also revealed recently that unlike symmetric GBs, asymmetric GBs may show less-predictable mobilities [38]. On the other hand, according to our MD calculations for the three FCC metals, GB energies of TD0 can be twice larger than that of TD1 (Table 2). Different mobilities exhibited by TD0 and TD1 are therefore well-expected.
main_letter.34: No such difference was observed for W-P crystals with Σ5 twin Tets. In this case, Dod0 and Dod1 are bounded by $\{ 2 \overline { { { 1 0 } } } \} / \{ 0 1 0 \}$ TD0-type GBs and $\{ 0 1 0 \} \ / \ \{ 0 0 1 \}$ TD1-type GBs, respectively. Our MD calculations show that the variation of GB energies is merely $1 0 ~ \%$ between pure-twist and symmetric tilt Σ5 planar GBs (Table 3). In other words, GB energies between Tets and Dods fulfill more closely the isotropic GB criterion [36] such that the evolution of the entire GB network into that of a Euler crystal can be ruled out.
main_letter.36: Our MD observations suggest that, in addition to zero-mean curvature characteristic of Euler catenoids underlying vanishing surface tension or capillary forces driving GB migration according to the Young-Laplace law [12,36], twin products due to GB reactions at TLs and QPs also contribute to stabilize the GB network of a Euler crystal at equilibrium.
main_letter.37: GBs between Tet pairs can also be put into two categories, approximately, which can be visualized as shown in Figs. 5 and 7. Such minimalsurface GBs follows lattice orientations of typical Σ9 GBs, such as A [122] [212] [221] / D [122] [212] [221] or I [184] [814] [447] / D [122] [212] [221] [36]. Those Σ9 GBs not only possess different excess energies in different GB normal (supplemental Table S2), but also are capable to decompose into twins (supplemental Fig. S2). Specifically, a Σ9 GB is marked by ‘Σ9a’ if GB reactions into twins may occur more readily; otherwise, it is marked by ‘Σ9b’.
main_letter.38: According to the well-known Herring’s rule [39], the balance of GB energies at any point of an equilibrium GB triple line in terms of the dihedral angles (θ) is given by
main_letter.40: Therefore, it requires a mutual compromise of GB energies, dihedral angles and the position of triple junctions to stabilize a TL out of equilibrium. Otherwise, additional activities such as thermally activated GB reactions may operate upon which a TL may dissociate, e.g., according to $\Sigma 9 a + \Sigma 9 a + \Sigma 9 a {  } \Sigma 9 ^ { \prime } + \Sigma 3 + \Sigma 3 ^ { \prime } + \Sigma 3 ^ { \prime \prime } [ 2 , 6 ] .$ QPs associated with Dod1 act as energetically favorable sites to trigger GB reactions as observed in our MD simulations (Fig. 3 and 4).
main_letter.41: Different combinations of Σ9a and Σ9b GBs thus can be assigned for a given chain, such that the twin decomposition is more readily to occur if a chain is formed by more than two Σ9a GBs (e.g., a-a-a and a-a-b) and less readily to occur if it is formed by more than two Σ9b GBs (e.g., b-b-a and b-b-b). As illustrated in Fig. 7, such a combination rule indeed applies when additional twins are produced at elevated temperatures in our MD simulations.
main_letter.42: # 5.3. Thermal stability: MD vs. experimental data
main_letter.43: The MD measured size-dependent thermostability for W-P crystals and Eulers crystals of Cu, Al and Ni have been plotted in Fig. 8. Although the transformation from W-P to Euler crystal has been observed for all the three metals, the reduced grain coarsening temperatures are not equivalent. Such a material dependence can be traced back to different bonding characteristics in different FCC metals. In particular, in terms of stable vs. unstable stacking fault energies [26], symmetric vs. asymmetric GB energies and the orientation-dependent Young’s and shear moduli [36], both Cu and Ni are intrinsically more anisotropic than Al. Clearly, the isotropic characteristic of Al can be revealed in Fig. 8.
main_letter.44: Several pieces of experimentally observed grain coarsening data [13, 40,41] are also included in Fig. 8. The reduced grain coarsening temperatures suggest that enhanced thermal stability can be obtained with grain sizes below $1 0 0 ~ \mathrm { { n m } }$ in experiments. The trend revealed by extension of our MD data agrees reasonably well with the experimentally observed outstanding thermostability in the nanometer regime. According to our MD data, topologically arrested GB network and promoted thermal stability of polycrystals are closely relevant and conceivable for nanograined polycrystals.
main_letter.46: We summarize our results and conclude as following.
main_letter.47:
(i) Inspired by W-P geometry, the polycrystalline structure of Cu was modeled by mixing two types of grain cells of equal volume but different shapes, tessellating space with an optimized global minimization of GB area. Vertical CTB networks (Voronoi) were introduced to clarify their roles on the thermal stability of W-P crystals at the grain size limit below 10 nm via MD simulations.   
(ii) Structural evolution of W-P crystals into Euler crystals characterized by catenoids with minimal-surface GBs were observed, which occurs in W-P crystals with Σ3 {111} twin Tets. The transformation ensures optimized GB morphologies both structurally and energetically, resulting in metastable states of polycrystalline microstructures at equilibrium.   
(iii) The structural transformation depends on equilibrium conditions of surface tensions (GB energies), balanced by GB migration as well as GB reactions to produce additional twins, preferentially at GB nodes and chains (QPs and TLs). The scenarios suggest that Σ9 related asymmetric GBs are in general highly adaptative in FCC polycrystals.   
(iv) Twin boundary networks and their distribution profiles are essential to promote the transformation to topological minimalsurface structures. The ultimate thermal stability of a GB network relies on CTB locking and fencing effects and hence depends on the extent of enclosed twins.
main_letter.48: (v) Besides of the prototype minimal-surface structure found previously based on Kelvin conjecture, i.e., the diamond Schwarz crystals (D-SCs) [12], our MD simulations provided further evidence that the presence of minimal-surface GB in other topological forms, such as Euler catenoid, is also possible.
main_letter.49: Achieving desired stability of microstructure is key issue for materials engineering [42–45]. Proper combination of grain lattice orientations is essential to stabilize the microstructure of nanograined polycrystals. Promoting microstructural evolution into Schwarz crystals including Euler catenoids through texture-selective processing techniques can be considered as a new strategy to optimize and design polycrystalline metals and alloys with improved thermal stabilities for various engineering purposes.
